food_subgroup: Prepared vegetable dish with condiment dressing

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Lao Beijing Jie Mo Dun (mustard-dressed napa cabbage). FOODON_03301710 (Chinese cabbage food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers raw Chinese cabbage as a commodity. No specific code exists for the prepared mustard-dressed Beijing dish; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this traditional Beijing prepared vegetable dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Lao Beijing Mustard Chinese Cabbage is approximately 200 g, presented as layered napa cabbage rolls dressed with mustard sauce, served cold as a starter or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese cabbage (pe-tsai), raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11119; mustard condiment contribution estimated from USDA FoodData Central #02046
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw Chinese cabbage (pe-tsai, FNDDS #11119 / FDC ID 169975) as the primary ingredient, representing approximately 85% of dish weight. Mustard dressing (Chinese-style, approximately 10% dish weight) contributes additional sodium, fat, and minor energy; vinegar and sugar contribute minor carbohydrate. Final per-100g values are weighted estimates reflecting the prepared dish composition. No direct USDA or FAO entry exists for the complete prepared dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; Chinese cabbage / Brassica vegetables analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lao Beijing Jie Mo Dun. GI of 32 is estimated by analogy with non-starchy Brassica vegetables (cabbage, GI ~10–40 range) and the added sugar and vinegar in the dressing. The University of Sydney GI Database lists cabbage and similar non-starchy vegetables in the low-GI category (GI < 55). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (32 × 5.2 × 2.0) / 100 = 3.3, confirming very low GL. This is a conservative upper-bound estimate; actual GI may be lower.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from mustard dressing and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 200 g restaurant serving provides approximately 560 mg sodium, representing about 24% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients managing blood pressure should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 32 and a glycemic load of approximately 3.3 per serving, this dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as part of a balanced meal, though the added sugar in the dressing should be noted and portion-controlled if blood glucose management is a priority.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and Brassica phytonutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat (0.1 g per 100 g) and provides dietary fibre and glucosinolates from napa cabbage, which are associated with cardiovascular protective effects; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention for patients with heart failure or sodium-restricted cardiovascular diets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lao Beijing Jie Mo Dun (老北京芥末墩, Old Beijing Mustard Chinese Cabbage) is a classic traditional cold appetiser from Beijing cuisine, dating back to imperial-era Manchu-Han banquet traditions. The dish is prepared by blanching napa cabbage (Chinese cabbage, Brassica rapa subsp. pekinensis) leaves, which are then layered and rolled into compact cylindrical bundles (dun'er, 墩儿) and dressed with a pungent homemade Chinese mustard sauce (芥末, jiè mò) combined with rice vinegar, sugar, and salt. The mustard used is a sharp, sinus-clearing yellow mustard paste made from ground mustard seeds, distinct from Western yellow mustard. The dish is served cold and is prized for its refreshing, piquant, and slightly sour flavour profile, with a crisp-tender texture from the blanched cabbage. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-fat, high-moisture vegetable dish providing approximately 38 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g), vitamin C (27 mg/100 g), folate (66 mcg DFE/100 g), and potassium (238 mg/100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 32 (low), making it appropriate for glycaemic management. Sodium content is moderate at approximately 280 mg per 100 g due to the seasoned dressing, which is a consideration for hypertensive individuals. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and halal. It is culturally significant as a symbol of old Beijing culinary heritage and is commonly served as a cold starter (凉菜, liáng cài) during festive occasions, banquets, and family gatherings in northern China.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Lao Beijing Jie Mo Dun exists in any approved primary regional or global nutrient database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw Chinese cabbage (the dominant ingredient) with weighted estimation for the mustard dressing contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database category data for non-starchy Brassica vegetables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #169975 (Chinese cabbage, raw) as the primary ingredient base, with weighted adjustments for mustard dressing, vinegar, and sugar contributions. No direct approved-source entry exists for the complete prepared dish. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero for this plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero; dish is entirely plant-based). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; dish contains no animal products). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; no animal-derived or fortified ingredients present). 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated at 0.5 g from mustard seed oil fraction). polyunsaturated_fat_g estimated at 0.5 g. All -1 values in the per_100g block are not present; all micronutrients have been estimated or confirmed from USDA source data. 4. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 5. Sodium content (280 mg/100 g) reflects seasoned prepared dish; raw Chinese cabbage sodium is approximately 9 mg/100 g per USDA, with the remainder attributable to mustard, salt, and vinegar in the dressing. 6. The dish is served at See Ya Tonight (今晚有局) restaurant in Singapore at SGD 15.34 per portion. 7. FoodOn match FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific prepared dish.
